main_ingredients: pastry shell (wheat flour, shortening or butter)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard egg tart with red bean and sago filling weighs approximately 75 g and constitutes a typical single-serve dessert portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Tart — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for standard egg tart (plain custard). The red bean paste and sago filling components were estimated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sweetened red bean paste (USDA FDC ID 169957) and sago (USDA FDC ID 169717), blended proportionally with the HPB egg tart base. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values reflect a weighted composite of approximately 55% egg tart base, 30% sweetened red bean paste, and 15% cooked sago pearls. All secondary nutrient values (fibre, sugar, sodium, saturated fat) are similarly estimated from this composite. The input record's per_100g values (energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g) are internally inconsistent with a pastry-based dessert containing egg custard, red bean paste, and sago — these values appear to be erroneous or misassigned. Corrected values are derived from the HPB/USDA composite as documented.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Egg Tart and Analogous Pastry-Custard Desserts — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg tart with red bean and sago filling. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous pastry-custard desserts in the University of Sydney GI Database (custard tart GI ~65–70) and the known GI of sago (GI ~70, high) and sweetened red bean paste (GI ~40–50, low-medium). The composite GI of approximately 65 reflects the dominant contribution of refined wheat pastry and sago starch, partially offset by the protein and fat content of the egg custard. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 27.4) / 100 ≈ 17.8 for a 75 g serving (carbohydrate_g = 36.5 × 0.75 = 27.4 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g) triggers the AHA added-sugar gate for hypertension; trans fat from shortening may also contribute to endothelial dysfunction.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit egg tart with red bean and sago filling due to its high sugar load (18 g/100 g) and low fibre (1.5 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevates blood pressure; sodium at 120 mg/100 g is moderate but does not offset the sugar-driven risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (18 g/100 g) exceeding the 15 g/100 g diabetes hard gate, combined with high refined carbohydrate (36.5 g/100 g), low fibre (1.5 g/100 g), and a medium-high estimated GI of 65, producing a glycemic load of approximately 17.8 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this dessert or limit to very small occasional portions, as the combination of sweetened red bean paste, egg custard, refined pastry, and sago pearls creates a high glycemic load (GL ≈ 17.8 per 75 g serving) with minimal fibre to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.2 g/100 g, estimated trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g from shortening, cholesterol at 85 mg/100 g, and high sugar (18 g/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dessert due to its saturated fat (4.2 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g from pastry shortening), and high sugar content (18 g/100 g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and endothelial health; occasional small portions only are advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from shortening triggers the RA trans fat pro-inflammatory hard gate; sugar at 18 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dessert, particularly during flares, as trans fat from pastry shortening and high sugar content (18 g/100 g) promote systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Tart with Red Bean and Sago Filling (红豆西米蛋挞) is a Singaporean dessert that combines a baked shortcrust or flaky pastry shell with a smooth egg custard filling, sweetened red bean paste, and chewy translucent sago pearls. The pastry shell is made from wheat flour and shortening or butter, giving it a crumbly, buttery texture. The egg custard layer is silky and lightly sweet, made from eggs, milk, and sugar. The red bean paste — typically sweetened adzuki bean paste — adds a dense, earthy sweetness, while the sago pearls contribute a soft, gelatinous chewiness. This dessert is commonly found in Singaporean bakeries, dim sum restaurants, and hawker centres, and is popular as an afternoon snack or dessert. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at approximately 248 kcal per 100 g, with significant carbohydrate (36.5 g/100 g), moderate fat (9.5 g/100 g including 4.2 g saturated fat and an estimated 0.1 g trans fat from shortening), and moderate protein (4.8 g/100 g). Sugar content is high at 18 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g), resulting in a medium estimated glycemic index of 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 17.8 per standard 75 g serving. This dessert is not recommended for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar, saturated fat, and estimated trans fat content.

confidence_reason: The input record's per_100g macros (energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g) are internally inconsistent with a pastry-based dessert containing egg custard, red bean paste, and sago — these values are almost certainly erroneous or misassigned from a different food. Corrected nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of HPB Foodo egg tart data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sweetened red bean paste and sago, yielding medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate. A high-confidence rating cannot be assigned until direct nutrient analysis of this specific product is available.
notes: 1. INPUT DATA ANOMALY: The input per_100g values (energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g) are nutritionally implausible for an egg tart with red bean paste and sago filling. A pastry-based dessert of this type would be expected to have energy >200 kcal/100 g, fat >5 g/100 g, and carbohydrate >30 g/100 g. These input values appear to be misassigned from a different food record and have been replaced with composite estimates. 2. FAT CORRECTION: The input fat_g value of 0.0 g is not naturally zero for this food — egg tart pastry shells contain shortening or butter, and egg custard contains fat from eggs and milk. Fat_g of 0.0 g in the input is an error, not a confirmed zero. The corrected fat_g of 9.5 g/100 g is estimated from the HPB/USDA composite. 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1.0 as detailed micronutrient data for this specific composite dessert are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on the likely use of partially hydrogenated shortening in the pastry shell; this is an estimate and should be confirmed by product-specific ingredient analysis. 5. GI ESTIMATE: Glycemic index of 65 is an analogous estimate based on custard tart and sago GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI FOODON_03315091 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are approximate matches for egg tart; no specific ontology code exists for the red bean and sago variant.
